title: Thin-File Synthetic Ramping with Initial Low-Value Seasoning Period
narrative: A synthetic identity establishes credit history through 3-4 months of small authorized user tradelines, then receives first-party credit card with $500-$2000 limit. Pattern shows deliberate seasoning: 6-8 weeks of micro-transactions ($3-$15 gas stations, fast food) with perfect payment history to build TransactionAmt_card1_mean baseline below $20. Account suddenly shifts to maximum utilization phase with 12-18 transactions over 48-72 hours, all near credit limit, targeting high-liquidity MCCs (electronics, jewelry, gift cards). Velocity spike shows card1_txn_count_24hr jumping from 0.2 average to 8-12. Email domain switches from free provider used during application to burner domain for final bust-out purchases. Delivery addresses fragment across 3+ zip codes (high addr1_entropy) despite thin credit file showing single address history. No chargebacks filedâ€”classic bust-out with no intent to dispute.
indicators: card1_txn_count_1day jumps from 0-1 baseline to 8-12 transactions within 48-hour window
TransactionAmt_card1_mean historically $8-$18 for 40+ days, then 12+ transactions at $180-$450 range
P_emaildomain changes from gmail.com/yahoo.com to disposable domain (mailinator, guerrillamail) in final 72 hours
addr1 shows 3-4 distinct zip codes across final transaction burst despite single-address credit application
Account age 90-150 days with card1_total_txn_count under 45 before velocity spike
All bust-out transactions in MCC 5732 (electronics), 5944 (jewelry), 5815 (digital goods) within 3-day window
dist1 values exceeding 500km between consecutive transactions in 6-hour periods during final phase
summary: A newly established account maintains 6-8 weeks of tiny purchases under $20 with perfect payments, then suddenly executes 8-12 high-value transactions near the credit limit within 48-72 hours across multiple distant locations. The email switches to a disposable domain and shipping addresses fragment across 3+ zip codes during this final burst.
reasoning: The fraudster deliberately builds a clean payment history to gain trust and credit increases, then executes a coordinated bust-out—maxing out the card with no intention of repayment. The sudden geographic spread, disposable email, and concentration in high-resale categories like electronics and gift cards reveal this is orchestrated theft rather than legitimate spending.
indicator_explanations: Card suddenly erupts from dormant to frantic activity within two days
Spending jumps twentyfold after weeks of small purchases building fake history
Email switches to burner service right before the bust-out attack begins
Shipping addresses scatter across multiple zip codes during final spending spree
Card stayed quiet with minimal activity for months then exploded overnight
All final purchases hit high-resale categories like electronics and jewelry fast
Transactions ping-pong across impossible distances during the final cashout phase
